topic: How this Raspberry Pi decides which garden bed to water
voiceover_script: This garden waters itself, and the brain behind it costs seventeen pounds. Most watering systems run on a timer. They flood beds after rain and leave them dry in a heatwave, because a clock doesn't know what the soil actually needs. This one reads the soil instead. A capacitive moisture sensor sits in each bed, wired through an analogue-to-digital board so the Pi can actually understand it. Every thirty minutes, the Pi checks all five beds. Any bed below its moisture target gets its valve opened and the pump switched on, one bed at a time. It stops the moment the soil is wet enough, or after a safety timeout — whichever comes first. No flooding, no guessing. The whole thing runs off one small solar panel, and it won an engineering award for exactly this reason. Full build plans, parts list and the actual code — link in bio.

scenes.text: This garden waters itself, and the brain behind it costs seventeen pounds.
Most watering systems run on a timer. They flood beds after rain and leave them dry in a heatwave, because a clock doesn't know what the soil actually needs.
This one reads the soil instead. A capacitive moisture sensor sits in each bed, wired through an analogue-to-digital board so the Pi can actually understand it.
Every thirty minutes, the Pi checks all five beds. Any bed below its moisture target gets its valve opened and the pump switched on, one bed at a time.
It stops the moment the soil is wet enough, or after a safety timeout — whichever comes first. No flooding, no guessing.
The whole thing runs off one small solar panel, and it won an engineering award for exactly this reason.
Full build plans, parts list and the actual code — link in bio.
